title: Famous Cat Owners in History 🐱 From Lincoln to Hemingway
description: Abraham Lincoln, Mark Twain, Florence Nightingale, Winston Churchill — history's brightest minds shared their lives with cats. Discover the surprising stories of presidents, authors, and scientists who couldn't function without their feline companions. Real biographical sources.

segments.voice: Some of the most influential people in human history shared a single quiet habit. They lived with cats. Presidents, authors, scientists, generals — pulling back the curtain on their private lives reveals a surprising amount of fur on the furniture and paw prints across history.

Welcome to CatBrothers. Let's start with Abraham Lincoln. He's widely considered the first U.S. president to bring cats into the White House. His cat Tabby was reportedly so spoiled that Lincoln once fed him with a gold fork during an official dinner. His wife Mary was not amused.

Lincoln also had Dixie, another cat he openly preferred over his cabinet members. He was once quoted as saying Dixie was smarter than his entire cabinet. The Lincoln biographer Carl Sandburg documented several of these incidents, painting a picture of a president who quietly adored cats.

Mark Twain may have been the most cat-obsessed author in American literature. He owned dozens over his lifetime, with names like Apollinaris, Beelzebub, Buffalo Bill, Sour Mash, and Soapy Sal. Twain frequently rented additional cats during summer trips because his own cats stayed home.

Twain wrote that a home without a cat — a well-fed, well-petted, properly revered cat — may be a perfect home, but how can it prove its title. The quote shows up in countless cat lover circles to this day. He understood the assignment.

Florence Nightingale, the founder of modern nursing, owned more than 60 cats throughout her life. She was deeply attached to them, and according to her biographer Edward Cook, she often wrote with a cat curled on her papers. Her favorites included Mr. Bismarck, named after the German chancellor.

Nightingale's correspondence frequently mentioned her cats by name. She believed cats provided psychological comfort to bedridden patients and discussed this in her notes on hospital design. Decades before pet therapy became formal medicine, she was already documenting the calming effect of feline presence.

Winston Churchill's relationship with his cat Jock is well documented. Jock, a marmalade tabby gifted to Churchill on his 88th birthday, became inseparable from the prime minister. Churchill insisted Jock attend every meal at his estate, Chartwell, and his will included a clause specifying Chartwell must always house a marmalade cat named Jock.

To this day, the Chartwell estate honors that clause. The current resident is Jock VII, the seventh ginger cat to hold the position. The British National Trust manages the property and rotates Jocks as needed. Few last wishes have been so faithfully maintained across multiple generations.

Ernest Hemingway's love of cats is legendary. His Key West home in Florida hosted dozens of cats, many of them polydactyl — meaning they had extra toes. His original cat, Snow White, was given to him by a ship captain. Today, the property still houses 40 to 50 descendants.

Polydactyly was considered good luck among sailors, who valued cats with extra toes for better grip on rolling ships. Hemingway brought that maritime tradition into his home. The Hemingway House in Key West is now a museum, and visiting the cats has become a major part of the experience.

Nikola Tesla credited a cat with sparking his fascination with electricity. As a child in Croatia, his black cat named Macak gave him a static electricity shock when he petted him. Tesla later wrote that the moment ignited his lifelong obsession with electrical phenomena.

Tesla wrote — I am quite unable to express the trend of feeling which the strange phenomenon awakened in me. The story is included in his autobiography My Inventions. The cat that helped invent the modern world wasn't just a pet — it was an early science teacher.

Edgar Allan Poe owned a tortoiseshell cat named Catterina. She reportedly slept on his wife's chest during her illness, possibly to provide warmth. Poe later wrote The Black Cat, partially inspired by his own complicated relationship with feline imagery and superstition.

Charles Dickens owned a cat named Bob, who often interrupted his writing by snuffing out his candle with a paw. When Bob died, Dickens reportedly had his cat's paw preserved as a letter opener — a strange Victorian custom that survives in museums today. Devotion can take unusual forms.

Sir Isaac Newton allegedly invented the cat flap. While not all historians agree, the story claims Newton cut a hole in his door so his cat could pass through without disturbing his work. A second smaller hole was cut for the kittens. Whether true or apocryphal, it's a cherished bit of cat folklore.

T.S. Eliot wrote Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats in 1939, a collection of poems about cats with names like Mungojerrie, Macavity, and Mr. Mistoffelees. The book later inspired the musical Cats. Eliot's correspondence revealed he genuinely believed every cat needed three names.

Pope Leo XII reportedly raised a cat named Micetto inside the Vatican. The cat had access to most areas of the papal apartments, sometimes interrupting official meetings. After the pope's death, Micetto was given to François-René de Chateaubriand, who took him back to France.

Cardinal Richelieu, the powerful 17th century French statesman, kept 14 cats in his palace. He even left his cats a stipend in his will, ensuring they'd be cared for after his death. The cats were named individually — Lucifer, Felimare, Soumise, and others — each with their own caretaker.

Florence Nightingale wasn't alone among nurses. Many wartime hospitals kept cats for both rodent control and patient comfort. The military tradition of ship cats continued through both World Wars. Cats served on Royal Navy vessels until 1975, when the practice was officially ended.

Salvador Dalí walked an ocelot named Babou through Paris and New York. While not strictly a domestic cat, Babou attended dinners at expensive restaurants and once startled a woman who mistook the wild cat for a regular pet. Dalí calmly told her it was a normal cat painted to look exotic.

Modern presidents continue the tradition. Bill Clinton's cat Socks became one of the most photographed pets in White House history. Joe Biden's family added Willow, a tabby, to the White House in 2022. The pattern repeats — leadership and cats keep finding each other.

What's striking about these stories isn't the fame. It's the consistency. Across centuries, cultures, languages, and political systems, brilliant minds have chosen to share their homes with cats. The pattern hints at something — cats may attract people who think differently, deeply, and quietly.

Modern psychological research suggests introverts and creative thinkers tend to gravitate toward cats. A 2014 study from Carroll University found cat owners scored higher on intelligence tests than dog owners. The sample was small, the conclusions debated, but the pattern keeps appearing in different studies across decades.

So the next time your cat refuses to move from your keyboard, remember — they're following a long literary tradition. Mark Twain dealt with the same. So did Hemingway. So did Tesla. You're in extraordinary company, fur on the laptop and all.

Subscribe to CatBrothers and hit the bell. What's coming next week — how cats helped humans survive the medieval Black Death and why the historical record may have it backwards. See you in the next documentary.
